54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74

76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

158	## Rules

161	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
162	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
163	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.

Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.1.c sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It upgrades the set-valued
7	% relative Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
8	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) to its abelian-group-valued
9	% refinement and then to its \'etale sheafification
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\). The chapter is gated on
11	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b, pullback functor + set-valued
12	% quotient on disk) and on \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, the
13	% relative-spectrum machinery underlying any concrete Lean-side computation).
14	% It \emph{gates} A.2.c (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) since
15	% Grothendieck's existence theorem represents precisely the \'etale-sheafified
16	% functor (cf.\ [Kleiman], \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
17	% \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).''). It is \emph{not} responsible for representability
18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
149	
150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)

229	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where

285	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
286	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
287	
288	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
289	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
290	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
291	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
292	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
293	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
294	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
295	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
296	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
297

299	[\'Etale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf]
300	  \label{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
301	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}

306	  % for the REPRESENTING SCHEME, a different mathematical object). The
307	  % ``\lean{...}`` pin is updated accordingly. The §"Lean encoding" bullet 5

356	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}

359	  % Def.~df:Pfs --- the \'etale-sheafified functor \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) is named

368	  % topologies  by

371	  %  \end{dfn}"
372	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
373	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) is named together with the
374	  abelian-group-valued relative Picard functor, meaning the sheaf is taken in
375	  the category of abelian groups).}
376	  The \'etale sheafification
377	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification} is a
378	  sheaf with values in the category of abelian groups: the canonical morphism
379	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is a homomorphism of group
380	  presheaves, and for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) the value
381	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is naturally an abelian group whose structure
382	  maps make \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) a presheaf
383	  \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\).
384	\end{theorem}
385	
386	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a self-contained proof of
387	% this auxiliary statement --- it is the standard fact that the sheafification
388	% of a presheaf of abelian groups in a Grothendieck topology is again a
389	% presheaf of abelian groups (equivalently, the forgetful functor
390	% \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves filtered colimits, and the
391	% plus-construction is built from such colimits). The Lean side appeals to
392	% Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}\) machinery.
393	\begin{proof}
394	  
395	  The \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck topology, and the
396	  forgetful functor \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves and reflects the
397	  filtered colimits used by the plus-construction. Therefore the
398	  sheafification of a presheaf \(\mathcal{F} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\)
399	  in the underlying \'etale site agrees, after the forgetful functor to sets,
400	  with the sheafification of the underlying set-valued presheaf
401	  \(U \circ \mathcal{F}\); equivalently, the sheaf
402	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} = (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) is canonically a
403	  sheaf of abelian groups, the canonical morphism
404	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is a homomorphism of group
405	  presheaves, and the restriction maps are group homomorphisms. This is a
406	  standard consequence of the plus-construction with values in an algebraic
407	  category; on the Lean side it is the Mathlib pattern
408	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}\) applied with target category
409	  \(\AddCommGroup\).
410	\end{proof}

210	/-- **Pullback along the projection** `π_T : C ×_S T ⟶ T` of a relative
211	curve. Sends a *line bundle on `T`* — i.e. an `O_T`-module `N` equipped with a
212	proof `hN : IsLocallyTrivial N` — to the line bundle `π_T^* N` on `C ×_S T`,
213	induced by the `Scheme.Modules.pullback` functor on `Scheme.Modules` together
214	with the preservation step `IsLocallyTrivial.pullback` (Stacks 01HH).

216	iter-187: refined to consume the `OnProduct`-style subtype on the source: the
217	underlying carrier is the direct application of Mathlib's
218	`Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)` functor at `N`, and the
219	locally-trivial witness comes from the project-side helper

222	noncomputable def pullbackAlongProjection {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
223	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (N : T.Modules) (hN : IsLocallyTrivial N) :
224	    OnProduct πC πT :=
225	  ⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N,
226	    hN.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)⟩

228	/-! ## §3. Composition of pullbacks
229	
230	The composition law for line-bundle pullback (Stacks tag 01HG) says that for any
231	composable morphisms `f : X ⟶ Y, g : Y ⟶ Z` and any line bundle `M ∈ Pic(Z)`,
232	the natural isomorphism `(g ∘ f)^* M ≅ f^* g^* M` makes the pullback functor
233	into a (pseudo-)functor `Sch^op ⥤ Cat`.
234	
235	Specialised to the relative curve setting: given `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, the
236	base-change morphism `g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T` satisfies
237	`π_T ∘ g_C = g ∘ π_{T'}` (definition of the fiber product). Therefore on
238	line bundles `g_C^* ∘ π_T^* = π_{T'}^* ∘ g^*`, with the equality realised as the
239	canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors via the pseudo-functoriality
240	of `Scheme.Modules.pullback` (Mathlib `Modules.pullbackComp`).
241	
242	Blueprint reference: `lem:pullback_compose` (Kleiman §2; Stacks tag 01HG). -/
243	
244	/-- **Composition of line-bundle pullbacks** along the projection. For a base
245	scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, test objects `T, T'` over `S`
246	via `πT : T ⟶ S` and `πT' : T' ⟶ S`, and a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`
247	(encoded by the hypothesis `πT' = g ≫ πT`), set
248	`g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T`
249	(the base-change morphism, given by `pullback.map`). On the underlying
250	`Scheme.Modules.pullback` functors the two routes through the canonical
251	naturality square give canonically isomorphic objects:
252	```
253	            Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)
254	  T.Modules  ─────────────────────────────────────────────►  (C ×_S T).Modules
255	       │                                                              │
256	       │ Scheme.Modules.pullback g            Scheme.Modules.pullback g_C
257	       ▼                                                              ▼
258	 T'.Modules  ─────────────────────────────────────────────►  (C ×_S T').Modules
259	            Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT')
260	```
261	i.e. for any `N : T.Modules`,
262	```
263	(Modules.pullback g_C).obj ((Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N)
264	  ≅
265	(Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT')).obj ((Modules.pullback g).obj N).
266	```
267	
268	This natural isomorphism is the substantive content of the
269	"line-bundle pullback functor is well-behaved under composition" fact
270	(Stacks tag 01HG). At the level of the `OnProduct` carrier (which is a typed
271	`sorry` in iter-174; see §1) it descends to equality of isomorphism classes
272	`[g_C^* π_T^* N] = [π_{T'}^* g^* N] ∈ OnProduct πC πT'`.
273	
274	iter-186: closed via the chain
275	1. `Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g_C (pullback.snd πC πT)` (functor iso applied at `N`)
276	2. `Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr` for the equation `g_C ≫ snd πC πT = snd πC πT' ≫ g`
277	   (`pullback.lift_snd` on the `pullback.map` lift).
278	3. `(Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (pullback.snd πC πT') g).symm` (applied at `N`). -/
279	theorem pullback_pullback_eq {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
280	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T)
281	    (hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) (N : T.Modules) :
282	    Nonempty
283	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback
284	            (Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S)
285	              (by rw [Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp]) (by rw [Category.comp_id, hg]))).obj
286	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N) ≅
287	        (Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT')).obj
288	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj N)) := by
289	  set g_C : Limits.pullback πC πT' ⟶ Limits.pullback πC πT :=
290	    Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S)
291	      (by rw [Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp]) (by rw [Category.comp_id, hg])
292	  have key : g_C ≫ Limits.pullback.snd πC πT = Limits.pullback.snd πC πT' ≫ g :=
293	    Limits.pullback.lift_snd _ _ _
294	  refine ⟨?_⟩
295	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g_C (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).app N ≪≫ ?_
296	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr key).app N ≪≫ ?_
297	  exact ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT') g).app N).symm
298

301	namespace RelPicPresheaf

303	/-! ## §4. The relative Picard quotient (set-valued)
304	
305	The relative Picard presheaf `Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`
306	quotients the line-bundle group of the product by the subgroup pulled back from
307	the base. As a quotient of abelian groups it is a quotient set; equivalently,
308	the equivalence relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` is captured by a
309	`Setoid` on the line-bundle type `OnProduct πC πT`. The quotient
310	`Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` is the underlying set of the relative
311	Picard presheaf at `T`.
312	
313	The "preimage_subgroup" name follows the Kleiman/Stacks convention: the
314	quotient is by the preimage `π_T^* Pic(T) ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T)`, treated as a
315	subgroup (image of a group homomorphism); the project-side Lean encoding
316	extracts the *Setoid* that the quotient construction takes.
317	
318	Blueprint reference: `thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined` (Kleiman §2,
319	Def. `df:Pfs`). -/
320	
321	/-- **Well-definedness of the relative Picard quotient as a `Setoid`.**
322	
323	The equivalence relation on `OnProduct πC πT` defining the relative Picard
324	presheaf
325	```
326	Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)
327	```
328	is the relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`. This relation is reflexive
329	(`L ⊗ L⁻¹ = O_(C ×_S T) = π_T^* O_T ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`), symmetric (the inverse of
330	a line bundle in `π_T^* Pic(T)` is in `π_T^* Pic(T)`), and transitive
331	(`L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` and `L' ⊗ L''⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)` give
332	`L ⊗ L''⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`); hence is an equivalence relation, encoded by a
333	`Setoid` on `OnProduct πC πT`.
334	
335	The substantive content is the well-definedness of the quotient: the canonical
336	projection `OnProduct πC πT → Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` is the
337	set-theoretic underlying map of the relative Picard presheaf at `T`.
338	
339	iter-186: with `OnProduct := (pullback πC πT).Modules`, the carrier is the
340	modules category. We encode the **iso-class setoid** `L ~ L' ↔ Nonempty (L ≅ L')`.
341	This is a substantive simplification: it identifies isomorphic modules
342	(giving the underlying set of `Pic(C ×_S T)` once invertibility is imposed),
343	but does not yet quotient by the pullback subgroup `π_T^* Pic(T)`. The full
344	quotient requires a tensor-product structure on `Scheme.Modules` (Mathlib
345	ships `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but no monoidal structure on
346	`Scheme.Modules` directly at `b80f227`) plus an inverse for invertibles;
347	iter-187+: refine to `L ~ L' ↔ ∃ N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L ⊗ L'⁻¹ ≅
348	pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available. -/
349	noncomputable def preimage_subgroup {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
350	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) :
351	    Setoid (LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT) where
352	  r L L' := Nonempty (L.carrier ≅ L'.carrier)
353	  iseqv :=
354	    { refl := fun _ => ⟨Iso.refl _⟩
355	      symm := fun ⟨e⟩ => ⟨e.symm⟩
356	      trans := fun ⟨e₁⟩ ⟨e₂⟩ => ⟨e₁ ≪≫ e₂⟩ }
357	
358	/-! ## §5. Naturality in the test scheme — the relative Picard presheaf as a functor
359	
360	For a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S` (encoded by the equality `πT' = g ≫ πT`),
361	the base-change `g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T` induces a map
362	`g_C^* : Pic(C ×_S T) → Pic(C ×_S T')` on line bundles, which (by
363	`lem:pullback_compose` = `pullback_pullback_eq` above) sends the subgroup
364	`π_T^* Pic(T)` into `π_{T'}^* Pic(T')`. Therefore it descends to a unique map
365	on quotients
366	```
367	g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) ⟶ Pic^♯_{C/S}(T'),    [L] ↦ [g_C^* L],
368	```
369	making `Pic^♯_{C/S} : (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` a presheaf.
370	
371	For the iter-174 file-skeleton, we pin the **object-level** functorial action
372	(i.e. the induced map of quotient sets for a single test morphism `g`). The
373	full `Functor` packaging (with the identity / composition laws supplied as
374	`map_id`, `map_comp` fields) is iter-175+ work once `OnProduct` and the
375	`preimage_subgroup` carrier are unpacked.
376	
377	Blueprint reference: `thm:pullback_natural` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
378	functor" + Def. `df:Pfs`; Stacks tag 01HG). -/
379	
380	/-- **The relative Picard presheaf is functorial in the test scheme.**
381	
382	Object-level functorial action: for any morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, there
383	is a canonical map
384	```
385	g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) ⟶ Pic^♯_{C/S}(T')
386	```
387	of quotient sets, factoring `g_C^*` through `preimage_subgroup πC πT` and
388	`preimage_subgroup πC πT'`. On representatives, `g^♯ [L] = [g_C^* L]` where
389	`g_C := id_C ×_S g`. The factorisation through the quotient is well-defined
390	because `g_C^*` sends `π_T^* Pic(T) ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T)` into
391	`π_{T'}^* Pic(T') ⊆ Pic(C ×_S T')` (this is the commutative-square content of
392	`pullback_pullback_eq`).
393	
394	iter-186: with the iso-class setoid `preimage_subgroup`, `Quotient.lift`
395	on `L ↦ ⟦(Scheme.Modules.pullback g_C).obj L⟧` is well-defined because
396	functors preserve isomorphisms. The identity / composition laws giving the
397	full `Functor (Sch/S)^op ⥤ Set` packaging follow from `pullbackId` /
398	`pullbackComp` and are iter-187+ work (they currently aren't needed by any
399	downstream consumer). -/
400	noncomputable def functorial {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
401	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T) (hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) :
402	    Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT) → Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT') :=
403	  let g_C : Limits.pullback πC πT' ⟶ Limits.pullback πC πT :=
404	    Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S)
405	      (by rw [Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp]) (by rw [Category.comp_id, hg])
406	  Quotient.lift
407	    (fun L : LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT =>
408	      Quotient.mk (preimage_subgroup πC πT')
409	        (⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullback g_C).obj L.carrier,
410	          L.isLocallyTrivial.pullback g_C⟩ : LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT'))
411	    (fun _ _ ⟨e⟩ => Quotient.sound ⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullback g_C).mapIso e⟩)
412	
413	end RelPicPresheaf
414	
415	end Scheme
416	
417	end AlgebraicGeometry

1281	The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
1282	sense: given any family of  $S$-schemes $T_i$, we have
1283		$$\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).$$
1284	 Hence, given a covering family $\{T_i\to T\}$ in the Zariski topology,
1285	the \'etale topology, or any other Grothendieck topology on the category
1286	of $S$-schemes, there is no harm, when we consider the sheaf associated
1287	to $\Pic_X$, in working simply with the single map $T'\to T$ where
1288	$T':=\coprod T_i$.  And doing so lightens the notation, making for
1289	easier reading. Therefore, we do so throughout, calling $T'\to T$ simply
1290	a {\it covering\/} in the given topology.
1291	
1292	The absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$ is never a separated presheaf in
1293	the Zariski topology.  Indeed, take an $S$-scheme $T$ that carries an
1294	invertible sheaf $\mc N$ such that $f^*_T\mc N$ is nontrivial.  (For
1295	example, take $T:=\I P^1_X$ and $\mc N:=\mc O_{\!T}(1)$.  Then the diagonal
1296	map $X\to X\x X$ induces a section $g$ of $f_T$; that is, $gf_T=1_T$.
1297	Hence $f^*_T\mc N$ is nontrivial.)  Now, there exists a Zariski covering
1298	$T'\to T$ such that the pullback $\mc N|T'$ is trivial; here, $T'$ is
1299	simply the disjoint union of the subsets in a suitable ordinary open
1300	covering of $T$.  So the pullback $f^*_{T'}\mc N|X_{T'}$ is trivial too.
1301	Thus $\Pic_X(T)$ has a nonzero element whose restriction is zero in
1302	$\Pic_X(T')$.
1303	
1304	According to descent theory, every representable functor is a sheaf in
1305	the Zariski topology \emdash in fact, in the \'etale and fppf
1306	topologies as well.  Therefore, the absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$ is never
1307	representable.  So, in the hope of obtaining a representable functor
1308	that differs as little as possible from $\Pic_X$, we now define a
1309	sequence of three successively more promising relative functors.
1310	
1311	\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
1312	  The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
1313		$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
1314	 Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
1315	topologies  by
1316		$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
1317		\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
1318	 \end{dfn}
1319	
1320	We now have a sequence of five Picard functors, and each one maps
1321	naturally into the next.  So each one maps naturally into any of its
1322	successors.  If the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
1323	then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
1324	associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
1325	the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
1326	check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
1327	associated to the absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$, as well as to the
1328	relative Picard functor $\Pic_{X/S}$.
1329	
1330	Since $\Pic_{X/S}$ is not a priori a sheaf, it is remarkable that it is
1331	representable so often in practice.
1332	
1333	Note that, for every $S$-scheme $T$, each $T$-point of
1334	$\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$, or element of $\Pic_{(X/S)\fpqc}(T)$, is
1335	represented by an invertible sheaf $\mc L'$ on $X_{T'}$ for some
1336	fppf-covering $T'\to T$.    Moreover, there must
1337	be an fppf-covering $T''\to T'\x_T T'$ such that the two pullbacks of
1338	$\mc L'$ to $X_{T''}$ are isomorphic \emdash or to put it informally, the
1339	restrictions of $\mc L'$ must agree on a covering of the overlaps.

406	\begin{proof}
407	  
408	  The \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck topology, and the
409	  forgetful functor \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves and reflects the
410	  filtered colimits used by the plus-construction. Therefore the
411	  sheafification of a presheaf \(\mathcal{F} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\)
412	  in the underlying \'etale site agrees, after the forgetful functor to sets,
413	  with the sheafification of the underlying set-valued presheaf

415	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} = (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) is canonically a

417	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is a homomorphism of group

prose comments per directive).

## Mathlib API confirmation (via `lean_leansearch`)

- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.etaleTopology`
  : `CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme`
  — `abbrev` in `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Sites.Etale`, generated by
  `Scheme.etalePretopology`. ✓ confirmed.
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smallEtaleTopology`
  : `(X : Scheme) → CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology X.Etale`
  — the small étale site (per-object), not what we want for the
  global relative Picard functor. Documented for completeness.
- `CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.sheafification`
  : `(J : GrothendieckTopology C) → (D : Type) → [HasMultiequalizer]
     → [HasColimitsOfShape (J.Cover X)ᵒᵖ D]
     → Functor (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ D) (Sheaf J D)`
  — concrete plus-construction (in
  `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.ConcreteSheafification`).
- `CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.sheafify`
  : `(J : GrothendieckTopology C) → (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ D) → (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ D)`
  — underlying presheaf-level version.
- `CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf`
  : `(J : GrothendieckTopology C) → (A : Type) → [HasWeakSheafify J A]
     → Functor (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) (Sheaf J A)`
  — abstract sheafification, valid under a `HasWeakSheafify` instance
  (in `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Sheafification`). This is the
  preferred entry point for the A.1.c chapter once
  `HasWeakSheafify Scheme.etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat` is available.
- `CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.sheafification_obj`
  : `J.sheafification D ▸ obj P = J.sheafify P`
  — the bridge lemma identifying the concrete plus-construction with
  the abstract sheafification, used in the proof sketch for
  `etSheaf_group_structure`.
- `AddCommGrpCat`
  — category of abelian groups, `Type (u+1)` (in
  `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.Grp.Basic`), with
  `hasColimitsOfShape J AddCommGrpCat` for any small `J` (in
  `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.Grp.Colimits`), satisfying the
  filtered-colimit requirement of the plus-construction.

None. Existing chapter macros (`\Pic`, `\AddCommGroup`, `\Sch`,
`\Spec`, `\et`, etc.) already cover the new prose.

## Notes for Plan Agent

- **Mathlib `HasWeakSheafify` instance for big-étale + AddCommGrpCat.**
  The chapter now names the precise Mathlib API names but does not
  unilaterally assert that
  `HasWeakSheafify Scheme.etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat` is registered
  at the project's pinned Mathlib commit (b80f227). The fallback path
  is documented (concrete `sheafify` + `plusPlusSheaf` via the

  `ConcreteCategory` with `forget` preserving the relevant filtered
  colimits and multiequalizers). The prover for `RelPicFunctor.lean`
  should test the abstract path first and fall back to the concrete
  one if the `HasWeakSheafify` instance is missing; if missing, the
  Mathlib-build-mode follow-up is the natural place to add it.

- **Slice-vs-absolute site.** The chapter's prose throughout uses
  `(Sch/k)^{op}` (Kleiman's convention with `S = Spec k`); Mathlib's
  `etaleTopology` is the absolute big étale site on `Scheme`. The
  chapter now documents (in the new etSheaf bullet) that the slice
  is captured via `Over (Spec k)` + `CategoryTheory.Sites.Over`. If
  the prover discovers that the slice formulation requires the
  `GrothendieckTopology.over` construction to be wired specifically

  may be a small additional Mathlib-build-mode item — but the
  mathematical content (sheafification preserves the abelian-group
  target on any Grothendieck site under `HasWeakSheafify`) is
  unaffected.

- **No re-architecture.** Per the directive's out-of-scope rules, no
  re-architecture of the carrier abstraction was proposed and no
  other chapter / `.lean` file was touched.

None. The chapter accurately reflects Kleiman §2 + §3 and the
